speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two. We're doing an interview with survivor Michael Steinstock in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. Mr. Steinstock, we were talking before about how the-- the trouble began in your town. And you were telling me about the early days of the Nazis' arrival. You mentioned that very early during almost the first two weeks, you yourself were captured. Can you tell me that story, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I walk on the street in center. city. I didn't live in the center city. I want to go. And they catch me, all young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I walking the street. What do you mean how you doing?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were just taking a walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked. I want to see how the-- somebody said a Russian tank come to Germany. Of course, there's a pact, Germany with Russia. And in the street, not only Jewish boys, and take in Catholic University, but the students have a special [INAUDIBLE] and recognize them, take them, too. And there I'm 13 month.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, just tell me a little bit about what happened to you. You're on the street. And what happened next? Were you captured at-- did someone point a gun--

speaker: interviewee
text: Take to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Take to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did someone literally grab you? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The-- this was the army walked in the street. And the young people taken with the-- the rifles. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you taken to-- or on? Were you put on a truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked.

speaker: interviewee
text: So not so far from there, maybe a half kilometer. And take the people there. And-- and I stayed there 13 month. And then two officers come in and ask who understand German who--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm just going to stop you for a moment. Because we want to talk a little bit about what it was like being there. Now, your family did not know where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you ever see them again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. In those 13 months, you were brought initially to a town center. What had it been used for before?

speaker: interviewee
text: The army, the Polish army is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It looked like what with the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: A barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: A barracks, yes, barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very plain?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's clean, yeah. But the Polish army left. And then the German moved in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea how many people were with you in this barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I can tell you or not. No, no. I think about a few thousand people.

speaker: interviewer
text: A few thousand?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few thousand. I don't know how much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, the first day when you were just walking on the street, you were captured, and you were brought to this place, what was the first thing that happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody think they're going to kill you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, you know, it's not so good. They get you too much to [? full ?] to eat, you know. And it's very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the living conditions like? Did you live in a great big room? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this is a barrack. This a long room with-- with no beds. This is a for wood to make like-- I don't how in English--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a plain, wooden--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --shelf almost.

speaker: interviewee
text: Laid like this, shelf like this and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you share your shelf with anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hundred people on one shelf.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had your own little narrow shelf?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I [? want ?] [? out ?] to others, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get a blanket?

speaker: interviewee
text: I slept in my-- as I come. With mine-- no blanket.

speaker: interviewer
text: No blanket.

speaker: interviewee
text: No blanket.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the only clothes you had--

speaker: interviewee
text: Clothes what I had, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what was done in the beginning? Did you have any idea that you were now unable to leave? Were you being guarded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody tell you this. No. The German said, you go away from-- from here. You're going somewhere else. You understand what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they tell you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German not tell you, nobody where you going to take you. No. I am the lucky. it was the two officer asked me-- not asked me, no, everybody. Who know where they can buy [NON-ENGLISH]? That's a [INAUDIBLE] in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. But that was after a while. That was 13 months.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But whole time, the food was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it? What was the food? What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I get you-- in the morning, I get a one dry-- I don't English-- [NON-ENGLISH]. This is the dry bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some bread? Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And get your water. You can go to the-- to take your water and [INAUDIBLE] you eat. And that's twice a day. Sometime get you a-- a soup. Nobody can eat this, you know? And this is the life in camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you spend your time? What did you do during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: You sitting in the-- there-- inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't work.

speaker: interviewee
text: No work. Because there's a lot of places that have to clean up, heavy physical work. And there-- I'll explain to you. It's called in Polish a [NON-ENGLISH]. In Jewish, too, a [POLISH]. This was the length in this room. And there are a lot of stones inside that you wash your shirts on, the shirts. You put in there and just pressed very heavy.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's something like laundry? Doing--

speaker: interviewee
text: A laundry. And this, have to remove from there. It was the half of the pump you have to remove. And the steps, there the steps, that the Polish army when she left there, she damaged the steps. You know, no steps to take from the second floor and the third floor down. To take that little wood, you know, you put [INAUDIBLE]. This terrible. You can-- lot of [INAUDIBLE] killed there. You can't run there. You have to walk very slowly. If you run, kills you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were you part of the workforce--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Working in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions? Was it very cold? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Is cold. In the winter time--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because it was winter by the--

speaker: interviewee
text: In winter time it's very cold. The Germans don't care for you if you're cold or not. You understand what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the shirts was everything is totally, you know [INAUDIBLE]. You have to throw out. They sent you to take a-- a shower. It's not a shower. Is a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's like a steam, like a steam. You have to steam. You get-- you give away the [INAUDIBLE] and you cloth and steam to stay in that room, you know, to-- to make the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so it sounds as if you were hungry a lot of the time--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --working very hard. And you're now 19 years old. Did you-- among the people who were there, did you talk together? Was there any time when you were not afraid? What was it like--

speaker: interviewee
text: You-- you talk when-- between-- you talk, yeah. Now, a lot of people, a lot of young people afraid to talk. Maybe you have something there, you know, listen to you. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there the fear that the people who were in the same situation as you were would betray you for a piece of bread? Or, was that happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody get a little bread and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I guess what I'm saying is--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know what you mean. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were all young. As the months went by, were you becoming weak? Were you losing weight?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I lost there about 25 pounds. I'm so skinny, [LAUGHS] like a twig.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that happened over time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And for everybody the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think about trying to get away, or was it impossible?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's an impossible. And this was like a miracle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe to me how you were guarded. Who was watching you? Were there soldiers with guns all around?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soldiers with-- yeah. Soldiers. You can't escape from there, impossible to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone try?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody tried. You cannot escape. Escape, you killed right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people die?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of people died.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they die from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Get typhus. You know typhus? Other sickness, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they died from being in this situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of people killed, too. Because you run, you try to go to the gates, killed you right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw people killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I saw this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see anyone killed who you cared about and knew, a friend or a relative?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a lot of people in Lublin they take from the street. Not only-- not only Jewish boys. You know, Catholic boys take, too. Because for him, this time, because the students from Catholic University killed 300 soldiers, [INAUDIBLE] 300 soldiers in Lublin, killed them. The students killed them. Now, to make [INAUDIBLE] [? lay down ?] next to the people, to the young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in this barracks you with some Catholic people who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I know you've been wanting to tell me how you escaped. And I'd love to hear that whole story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The two German officers comes in and ask who understand German and who know a store you can buy [NON-ENGLISH]. In German, it's [NON-ENGLISH]. I tell them I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that. I'm live in Lublin. I born there. And I now stores where you can find.

speaker: interviewer
text: Weren't there other people who would have known that also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a lot of people speak German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, OK. So you had the language advantage.

speaker: interviewee
text: And take me out in center city near Krakowskie Przedmiescie Street, was called Krakowskie. Thousands of people walk, because no cars. In my mind had to escape, you know? And there's a big post office. The post office like a one block the length. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you have a exit from Krakowskie Przedmiescie Street to the hospital street. You can go in. And you can go out of the hospital, the [NON-ENGLISH] now in Polish. And my mind work so fast. And I jump in-- in the post office. And the two German, tucked between him [INAUDIBLE]. And thousands of people on the street, we can choose nothing. I come out of the other street, hospital street called. And I escape to my friend, not to the house, not to my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew not go back, because they would look for you there? Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my friend called him Torffmann, the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you be able to spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you be able to spell his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, T-O-R-F-F-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was your friend or your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friend, young boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two brothers there. The people is a little richer, because the father have a horse with a wagon and make better like my father not. And I stayed there. And my-- I remember my father comes over [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] and give me his coat, because cold. He give me his coat, a black coat. And the sister bring me [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] black bread. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because I tell him, I [INAUDIBLE] to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- sorry, what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told my father and my sister I could escape to Russia. I live 75 kilometer from the Russian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just stop you for one second? Your father had not seen you in 13 months. Did he have any idea where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, in those 13 months that you were away, what had happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother cried. I thought so. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: But I mean, what was their life like? Were they able to exist? Were they able to get food? What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know nothing. Because there is coupons. Everybody get a coupon to get bread and something else, you know, for the families. And I escape. And I take the two brothers, Torffman, with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you immediately escape or you stayed with your--

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew you weren't going to stay, because it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- when I walk out in Lublin in the road, I find other boys and together 11 boys, Jewish boys. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I walk to Chelm. [CRYING] Chelm is 55 mile for Lublin, 55 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this was after the three days that you spent with your friends--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they came with you. Did you say goodbye to your family? Or, did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just--

speaker: interviewee
text: I told my father, my sister, my mother, I not see you. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I walk [CRYING] three days [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to the border. The border between Russia and German is a river called Bug. God.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bug was the river. And this side the German. And other side is the Russian. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember in Chelm everybody sleep over, the Jewish people, in the house--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were willing to take you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In the morning, 6 o'clock, I walked. I had over 20 kilometer to go. And I go-- it's called Dorohusk, a town. But everybody tell in Chelm-- Chelm us there you can come over the river to Russia. You know, you can go over. When I come to Dorohusk with my whole friend, the 11--

speaker: interviewer
text: The 11 of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The German solders very friendly, the soldiers said, you not can go over. Yesterday, closed the border. You have to go to town called Swierce. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] about 30 kilometer from Dorohusk. This is a village. And when I and my friends walk, it's so dark. And a Polish farmer said, boys, don't walk. But here, you have-- I don't know what in English what's called this. You have when you walk you can go in on the dirt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Quicksand maybe, or something you sink in?

speaker: interviewee
text: You sink in, yeah. And the two farmers take the 11 boys to the house overnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Jewish farmers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were not.

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't have Jewish farmers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you surprised by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- you see, in this place it's a very poor place for the Polish people. The farmers live very poor. And the farmers cooked potatoes for everybody. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were kind to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. 5:00 in the morning, I am with my friend. We walked to the Swierce, to the-- there's the headquarters for the German. You have to take a paper that can go over the river to Russian. And there you have a little boat. And the German soldiers or the Russian take you over. If you have in the German headquarters, a [NON-ENGLISH], in German called this, you can go over. I come there. Said, you not can go over. You have to go to the headquarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what to do? Because you have to tell him your lived before in Ukraine, in Polish Ukraine on the other side of the river, you can go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have papers. I have papers from Lublin. I belong to the German. And so I go to tell him-- everybody thought I lost everything-- the bombs. Because when I walked from Lublin to the border, the Germany killed the people in the-- in the-- in the roads. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And a little Polish boy about 12 years old comes over and said, I have a little boat. 5 zloty, that a $5-- and I had-- I take you over to the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of you? Or, just there were a couple of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six-- six can go in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have some money with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have, but the two brothers Torffman have the money. They pay the money. [LAUGHS] And I take them to the river. And nobody's there, no soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it daytime or nighttime?

speaker: interviewee
text: Daytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Daytime.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the river is a little frozen already. It's cold in November. There's very cold. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Before, got in 6. And I'm all the time they [INAUDIBLE]. I tell maybe the brothers, maybe something happened. [INAUDIBLE] the families, go over. Go over there. Come back with the boat, the boy. You take the other five together with me. In the middle of the river, the Germans start shoot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: I jump in the water. My luck, [INAUDIBLE] I'm not a swimmer.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know how to swim.

speaker: interviewee
text: I go like this in the water-- ice. You have ice. And thank God, I went over. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Was God--

speaker: interviewer
text: You suddenly learned how to swim? Or, you were able to just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the water it go to there.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was up to your--

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, I take my shoes off [INAUDIBLE] water. And the stocking get frozen right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And stayed maybe 10 minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the way, were you the only one? Or, were there others, the other five--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody got away from there already. Everybody is. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Maybe 10, 15 minutes, a-- a soldier with horse, a Russian soldier, comes over, takes everybody [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to a-- a little house. There are maybe 50 people in this house. A lot of communists escaped from the prisons, you know. I see straw on the floor. And people sleep there over at night. And one colonel said, I go send you back. I thought if you go send me back, I will kill myself here in this place. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I called somewhere. A lady come-- a soldier with a motorcycle. And everybody walked and walked. The name is the town Lubomil.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wouldn't know how to spell that, would you? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: L-U-B-O-M-I-L. Lubomil.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this is now, you're in Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And everybody in the house walked? Like, all 50 or so of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just your gang?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- the ones you had come with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Come there. There's the headquarters for the Russia. They said, you can go deeper Russia or send you back. [INAUDIBLE] before belonged to Poland, the land. The Russian take, in the war, take, [INAUDIBLE] and make it a part-- take half Poland, Ukraine-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The borders changed all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe a half day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you can go.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't think you to go back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I take your name and everything, you know, like that. They get you a paper. You walk. You walk to a station. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The station, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there I see a big train. A train is there, pullman train. You know what I what I mean, Pullman? Not passenger. This was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Freight? Freight cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Freight. And there are a lot of farmers, Polish farmers. The Russian take them all away with the cows, with the horses, everything on this train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I ask one farmer, how long you here? For three days. And my mazel-- you know what a mazel? Mazel is luck, uh, huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: The train, maybe an hour, the train moved. And I jumped up the step in the wagon. I don't-- everybody in my-- the 11 people. And I come to Kowel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, I'm sorry, you jumped onto the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: The train.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the train comes to Kowel. Kowel is K-O-W-E-L. This is, I'd say, a small city. In Kowel, you have thousands there, ten-- tens of thousands people, young people and older people escape from Poland, to Russia, from the whole Poland, around Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they, on the streets? In the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The people lived in the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's cold. And everybody-- I want go-- my friend, they looked here, how to sleep overnight. And I don't know where to go. I heard Michael. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I look. My neighbor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your?

speaker: interviewee
text: A neighbor.

speaker: interviewer
text: A neighbor.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Lublin. Said, what you do here? And I went, the same what you do here, escape, too, no? I told [INAUDIBLE] Mendel, the name, I don't have where to sleep. Come with me. He take me in a little room of above the school. And I have 20 people in a little like my kitchen, a little place. Now, one of brother sleep like this. You get eat in the restaurants for nothing. The Russian get you food for the people who escaped. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What a remarkable and lucky experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to probably have to stop right here for moment while we change the tapes. And we'll pick the story up soon.

speaker: interviewee
text: You change now?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just about to.
